6: A direct revelation: 'A Lamb standing, as though it had been slain, with seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth.' The Lamb-vision is Revelation's Christological center. The Lamb is: (1) slain — bearing the marks of the cross permanently in his resurrection body; (2) standing — the resurrection state; (3) with seven horns — omnipotent (seven = completeness; horns = power); (4) with seven eyes — omniscient. This is the highest Christological symbol in the canon: the crucified-and-risen Jesus is simultaneously the sacrificial victim and the omnipotent, omniscient Lord of all history. The entire drama of Revelation unfolds as this Lamb opens the seals of history.
12: A direct revelation: 'Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing!' The sevenfold doxology directed to the Lamb matches the sevenfold doxology directed to God in Revelation 5:13 — the deliberate parallelism attributes to Christ the same divine worthiness that belongs to YHWH alone. The ground of worthiness is the slaying (ho esphagmenos) — the cross is the qualification for cosmic lordship. The order in the throne-room is anti-imperial: not the emperor who receives tribute but the crucified-and-exalted Lamb who receives worship from every creature.
1: A direct revelation: in the right hand of the one on the throne is a scroll sealed with seven seals -- the title deed of creation and the plan of history's consummation. The scroll can only be opened by one who has the authority and qualifications. That no creature can open it (v3) and that only the Lamb can (v9) establishes the Christological claim: access to the meaning and direction of all history is held by Christ alone. The sealed scroll frames the entire cosmic drama that follows: who governs history, and by what right?
2: A direct revelation: 'A mighty angel proclaiming with a loud voice, Who is worthy to open the scroll and break its seals?' The cosmic question posed by the angel frames the central Christological inquiry of Revelation and of all history: who is qualified to direct history toward its appointed end? Worthiness requires both the authority of the sovereign and the qualifications of the redeemer. The question is posed publicly so that the answer -- only the Lamb -- is heard by every creature in heaven and earth and under the earth (v13).
3: A type fulfilled: 'No one in heaven or on earth or under the earth was able to open the scroll or to look into it.' The impossibility of any creature achieving what Christ alone achieves is the structural argument of Hebrews 9-10: no human priest's sacrifice, no angel's intercession, no created being's merit can accomplish the redemption and governance the scroll's opening represents. The comprehensive scope -- heaven, earth, under the earth -- establishes the total failure of all creaturely resources before the need that only the incarnate, crucified, and risen Son can meet.
4: A direct revelation: 'and I began to weep loudly because no one was found worthy to open the scroll or to look into it.' John's weeping is the representative grief of all creation for a locked history -- unredeemed, without a future, under the dominion of death and sin. The weeping that ceases in verse 5 when the Lion-Lamb is announced is the tears that cease at the cross and resurrection. Paul's language in Romans 8:19-22 -- creation groaning in futility -- is the cosmic correlate of John's weeping before the sealed scroll.
5: A direct revelation: 'Behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, so that he can open the scroll and its seven seals.' Two foundational messianic titles: Lion of Judah (Genesis 49:9-10: 'the scepter shall not depart from Judah') and Root of David (Isaiah 11:1,10: 'a shoot from the stump of Jesse'). His conquest (enikesen, aorist) is the cross and resurrection -- the counter-intuitive victory of dying to defeat death. John then turns to see the Lion and sees instead a Lamb standing as though slain (v6): the cross is how the Lion conquered.
7: A direct revelation: 'And he went and took the scroll from the right hand of him who was seated on the throne.' The Lamb's act of taking the scroll is the structural pivot of the entire book. This action transfers authority over creation's consummation to the one who qualified by his blood. John 17:2 ('you have given him authority over all flesh') and Daniel 7:14 ('to him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom') converge in this single act. Everything in Revelation 6-22 unfolds from the Lamb taking the scroll.
8: A direct revelation: 'the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb, each holding a harp, and golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints.' The transfer of worship from God alone in chapter 4 to the Lamb in chapter 5 is the most theologically dense moment of Revelation. The golden bowls of incense -- the prayers of the saints (Psalms 141:2) -- rise before the Lamb, who is their mediator and intercessor (1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them'). The prayers Christians offer are held by the crucified-risen Lord.
9: A direct revelation: 'Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation.' The new song (Psalms 33:3; Revelation 40:3; Revelation 96:1 -- new songs celebrate new acts of God) identifies the precise ground of the Lamb's worthiness: the substitutionary atonement. 'You were slain' and 'by your blood you ransomed' are the two clauses that qualify him for cosmic sovereignty. Universal in scope -- every tribe, language, people, nation -- this is the centripetal statement of Revelation's Christology.
10: A direct revelation: 'and you have made them a kingdom and priests to our God, and they shall reign on the earth.' The new song continues: Exodus 19:6 ('a kingdom of priests and a holy nation') is fulfilled by the blood of the Lamb from every nation. The vocation Israel failed to maintain under the old covenant is now constituted by Christ's redemption from every people. The reign 'on the earth' is both present (the church's priestly mediation) and eschatological (Revelation 20:4-6 -- co-reign with Christ). The Lamb's blood is the constitutional foundation of the priestly-royal church.
11: A direct revelation: 'Then I looked and heard the voice of many angels around the throne... numbering myriads of myriads and thousands of thousands.' The worship of the Lamb expands from the elders and living creatures to the full angelic host. Hebrews 1:6 ('when he brings the firstborn into the world, he says, Let all God's angels worship him') is enacted here: the angelic proclamation of Christ's worthiness is the heavenly fulfillment of the Father's command. The number -- innumerable -- suggests the totality of the heavenly court directing its worship toward the crucified-risen Lamb.
13: A direct revelation: 'And I heard every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, saying, To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever!' The doxology addressed equally to the Father and the Lamb establishes the Lamb's full divine status: what belongs to the Creator belongs also to the Redeemer. Philippians 2:10-11 ('every knee shall bow... every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord') is cosmically enacted here. The Lamb stands at the center of all creation's worship, jointly with the Father, forever.
14: A direct revelation: 'And the four living creatures said, Amen! and the elders fell down and worshiped.' The 'Amen' that closes the throne-room vision is the word Christ uses of himself in Revelation 3:14: 'the Amen, the faithful and true witness.' The living creatures' Amen to the cosmic doxology of verse 13 is, appropriately, the word that names Christ himself -- the affirmation that all God's promises find in him (2 Corinthians 1:20). The elders' prostration before the Lamb mirrors their prostration before the Father in Revelation 4:10, sealing the equality of their worship and the Lamb's full participation in the divine identity.
